coredata.dc:title: Silver nanoparticles decorated and tetraphenylethene probe doped silica nanoparticles: A colorimetric and fluorometric sensor for sensitive and selective detection and intracellular imaging of hydrogen peroxide
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Xinan
Zhou, Huipeng
Huang, Yumeng
Jiang, Hong
Yang, Na
Shahzad, Sohail Anjum
Meng, Lianjie
Yu, Cong
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we report a novel sensor for colorimetric and fluorometric H2O2 sensing which is based on silver nanoparticles decorated and tetraphenylethene probe doped silica nanoparticles (Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs). A positively charged tetraphenylethene (TPE) probe is doped into silica nanoparticles, and the nanoparticles exhibit strong fluorescence emission due to aggregation-induced emission (AIE) of the TPE probe. Ag nanoparticles (AgNPs) are prepared in situ on the surface of the silica nanoparticles. AgNPs serve as a nanoquencher which can quench the AIE emission of the TPE-SiO2 NPs efficiently. However, AgNPs can be oxidized to Ag+ by H2O2, which leads to fluorescence recovery and color fading of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs. The dual-readout strategy allows sensitive analysis of H2O2. The detection limit of the fluorometric and colorimetric assay is 0.28 and 2.1 μM, respectively. And the nanosensor also shows good selectivity. In addition, analysis of H2O2 in human serum and intracellular imaging of H2O2 are both demonstrated. With the good analytical properties of merit, the proposed nanoprobe has a promising potential for H2O2 related bioanalysis and biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tetraphenylethene
Aggregation-induced emission
Colorimetry
Turn-on fluorescence
H2O2 detection
Intracellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the polyethyleneimine modified tetraphenylethene doped silica nanoparticles (PEI@TPE-SiO2 NPs)
Synthesis of the silver nanoparticles decorated and tetraphenylethene probe doped silica nanoparticles (Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs)
Detection of H2O2 in human serum
ICP-MS measurements of cellular Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs
Synthesis and characterization of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs
MTT assay and intracellular imaging of H2O2

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21561162004 , 51761145102 , 21874128 ), the Science and Technology Development Project (International Collaboration Program) of Jilin Province ( 20160414040GH ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the TPE-SiO2 NPs (a) and the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs (b). Insets (a, b) are the size distribution of the TPE-SiO2 NPs and the AgNPs, respectively.
XPS analysis of (i) TPE-SiO2 NPs (black line), (ii) PEI@TPE-SiO2 NPs (red line) and (iii) Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs (blue line), (a) the survey XPS, (b) and (c): high resolution XPS.

(a) Fluorescence spectra changes of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs upon the addition of increasing concentrations of H2O2 (0, 1, 5, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 110, 120, 130, 140, 150, 160 μM). (b) Plot of fluorescence ratio (F/F0) against H2O2 concentration, F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity values at 475 nm of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs in the presence and absence of H2O2. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra changes of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs upon exposure to various concentrations of H2O2 (0, 5, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 140, 160 μM). Inset: photograph of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs in the presence of various concentrations of H2O2 (0, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 140, 160 μM). (d) Plot of absorption intensity at 413 nm versus H2O2 concentration.

Selectivity of the assay. (a) Fluorescence ratio (F/F0) changes of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs in the presence of other analytes. F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity values at 475 nm of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs in the presence and absence of H2O2. (b) Relative absorption intensity changes of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs in response to other analytes. A0 and A are the absorption intensity values at 413 nm of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs in the absence and presence of H2O2. Inset: photograph of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs in the presence of different analytes. Analyte concentration: Ag+, GSH and Cys 2 mM each; the others, 200 μM each.

Confocal fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells incubated with the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs and (a) no H2O2, (b) 10 μM H2O2, and (c) 100 μM H2O2. (d): HeLa cells were treated with PMA (1 μg/mL) first and then the fluorescence image was taken. (1) bright field image, (2) fluorescence image at the blue channel, (3) merged fluorescence and bright field images.
Synthesis of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs and the selective sensing of H2O2.

head.ce:title: Silver nanoparticles decorated and tetraphenylethene probe doped silica nanoparticles: A colorimetric and fluorometric sensor for sensitive and selective detection and intracellular imaging of hydrogen peroxide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Science and Technology of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun 130022, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we report a novel sensor for colorimetric and fluorometric H2O2 sensing which is based on silver nanoparticles decorated and tetraphenylethene probe doped silica nanoparticles (Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs). A positively charged tetraphenylethene (TPE) probe is doped into silica nanoparticles, and the nanoparticles exhibit strong fluorescence emission due to aggregation-induced emission (AIE) of the TPE probe. Ag nanoparticles (AgNPs) are prepared in situ on the surface of the silica nanoparticles. AgNPs serve as a nanoquencher which can quench the AIE emission of the TPE-SiO2 NPs efficiently. However, AgNPs can be oxidized to Ag+ by H2O2, which leads to fluorescence recovery and color fading of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs. The dual-readout strategy allows sensitive analysis of H2O2. The detection limit of the fluorometric and colorimetric assay is 0.28 and 2.1 μM, respectively. And the nanosensor also shows good selectivity. In addition, analysis of H2O2 in human serum and intracellular imaging of H2O2 are both demonstrated. With the good analytical properties of merit, the proposed nanoprobe has a promising potential for H2O2 related bioanalysis and biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) has been widely used in chemical, food and biomedical industries due to its strong oxidizing and reducing properties (Sitnikova et al., 2011; Maji et al., 2014).﻿ H2O2 is also one of the most important reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated in living organisms (Li et al., 2016; D’Autreaux and Toledano, 2007). Nevertheless, the overabundance of H2O2 is associated with serious disorders, such as neurodegenerative disorder, diabetes and cancer (Weinstain et al., 2014; Lippert et al., 2011). In this regard, establishing a sensitive and selective assay to detect H2O2 is of significant interest for applications in biology, biomedicine and environmental protection (Nossol and Zarbin, 2009).

Many laboratories have developed various strategies for H2O2 analysis such as the fluorescence (Weinstain et al., 2014), chemiluminescence (Yu et al., 2016), electrochemistry (Maji et al., 2014; Li et al., 2016) and colorimetry (Chen et al., 2014b) based methods. Among various sensors for H2O2, the colorimetric methods have drawn much attention because of the visible, simple and economical detection without any sophisticated instruments. However, in many cases the colorimetric method cannot be directly applied because of the low detection sensitivity and the strong background color of the assay medium (Kuo et al., 2015). To avoid this problem, fluorescence techniques have been widely used in many fields, for example fluorescence imaging of various analytes in cells, organisms and tissues (Sun et al., 2013). However, methods based on fluorescence quenching (turn off assay) can suffer interference from other quenchers and environmental condition changes (Yuan et al., 2014a). Consequently, it is an urgent need to develop a colorimetric and turn-on fluorometric dual-readout strategy for H2O2 analysis.

It is noteworthy that the silver nanoparticles decorated silica nanoparticles (Ag@SiO2 NPs) show more tailored and enhanced properties in plasmonics (Kang et al., 2013), photocatalysis (Chen et al., 2012) and antibacterial activity (Tian et al., 2014; Song et al., 2013) compared with that of colloidal silver nanoparticles. Herein, we designed new silver nanoparticles decorated and tetraphenylethene probe doped silica nanoparticles (Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs) for the colorimetric and fluorometric determination of H2O2 ( Scheme 1). Tetraphenylethene (TPE) is a typical aggregation-induced emission (AIE) luminogen. Unlike other aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) luminogens (Ma et al., 2016), TPE probe shows very weak emission in the monomeric state but is highly emissive in the aggregated state (Chen et al., 2014a, 2017; Leung et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2018). Thus, the TPE probe has a distinct advantage to be used as a doping dye in silica nanoparticles (Faisal et al., 2010), and in fact few reports have used TPE luminogen doped silica nanoparticles for sensing applications. In this paper, a positively charged TPE probe was doped into silica nanoparticles and showed strong fluorescence emission. Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) were then synthesized in situ on the silica nanoparticles. In this process, large size AgNPs (> 2 nm) were used as a nanoquencher (Li et al., 2015; Cao et al., 2014), and the fluorescence of TPE doped silica nanoparticles (TPE-SiO2 NPs) was quenched effectively by AgNPs due to the surface plasmon-enhanced energy transfer (SPEET) from the TPE-SiO2 NPs (donor) to the AgNPs (acceptor) (Ma et al., 2017). AgNPs could be etched by H2O2 and converted into Ag+. This kind of H2O2-induced AgNPs etching resulted in an increase in the fluorescence intensity (Li et al., 2015; Ma et al., 2017). Moreover, the conversion of the AgNPs to Ag+ exhibited a clear assay solution color fading, making it possible to detect H2O2 by a colorimetric assay (Chen et al., 2014b). Thus, a new colorimetric and turn-on fluorometric method for sensitive and selective detection of H2O2 based on the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs was established. And the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs were also used for the detection of H2O2 in human serum samples and intracellular imaging of H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the polyethyleneimine modified tetraphenylethene doped silica nanoparticles (PEI@TPE-SiO2 NPs)
ce:section.ce:para: Tetraphenylethene (TPE) probe was synthesized according to the literature procedures (Scheme S1, Figs. S1-S4, Supporting Information) (Chen et al., 2014a, 2017; Leung et al., 2013). The PEI@TPE-SiO2 NPs were prepared based on the Stӧber method (Huang et al., 2017a, 2017b). Briefly, 42.0 mL of ethanol, 4.0 mL of water, 0.5 mL of ammonium hydroxide (25%), 1.5 mL of TEOS and 2.0 mL of TPE probe (4 mM) were mixed and stirred for 24 h at room temperature (25 °C). Then following the addition of 0.5 mL of PEI (Mw = 1800, 86 mg/mL), the mixture was stirred for another 24 h at room temperature. The product was washed with ethanol and water several times. Finally, the PEI@TPE-SiO2 NPs were dried at 40 °C for 5 h in a vacuum oven.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the silver nanoparticles decorated and tetraphenylethene probe doped silica nanoparticles (Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs)
ce:section.ce:para: AgNO3 (5 mM) was mixed with the PEI@TPE-SiO2 NPs (10 mg/mL). The resulting mixture (1.0 mL) was stirred for 2 min, then 2 μL of formaldehyde solution (8 −14 wt%) was added. The solution was heated at 90 °C for 2 min in a water bath under continuous stirring and cooled slowly to room temperature. The color of the solution changed from white to brown. The Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs were washed twice with water and then redispersed in water at a concentration of 10 mg/mL.

The assay was optimized in terms of probe concentration (Fig. S14), buffer (Fig. S15) and reaction time (Fig. S16). The optimal detection conditions were adopted accordingly, 40 μL of Tris-HAc buffer (pH 7.4, 0.1 M), 30 μL of Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs (10 mg/mL), and different concentrations of H2O2 were mixed. The solution was diluted to 400 μL with water and mixed completely. After incubation for 30 min at room temperature (25 °C), UV–vis and fluorescence spectra were measured. Experimental conditions: Tris-HAc buffer (pH 7.4, 10 mM); 0.75 mg/mL probe; reaction time 30 min. The limit of detection (LOD) is defined as 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation of blank, k represents the slope of the linear regression equation.

For the selectivity test, 40 μL of Tris-HAc buffer (pH 7.4, 0.1 M), 30 μL of Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs (10 mg/mL), 200 μM H2O2 and the other analytes (Ag+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Hg2+, GSH, Cys, Glucose, ONOO−,·OH, TBHP, ClO−, O2 −) were mixed. These sample solutions were incubated for 30 min at an ambient temperature (25 °C), the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra were then collected. Experimental conditions: Tris-HAc buffer (pH 7.4, 10 mM); 0.75 mg/mL probe; analyte concentration: Ag+, NO2 −, GSH and Cys, 2 mM each; the others, 200 μM each.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of H2O2 in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: Human serum samples were obtained from the Third Hospital of Jilin University. The serum samples were treated by centrifugation with a 3000 molecular weight cut-off filter, 5% (v/v) methanol solution was then added to the filtrated serum samples, the samples were centrifuged at 8000 rpm (Jahanbakhshi and Habibi, 2016), and the supernatant was collected. 40 μL of Tris-HAc buffer (pH 7.4, 0.1 M), 30 μL of Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs (10 mg/mL), 40 μL of serum, and different concentrations of H2O2 were mixed. The solution was diluted to 400 μL with water and mixed completely. After incubation for 60 min at 60 °C, the UV–vis and emission spectra were collected. Experimental conditions: Tris-HAc buffer (pH 7.4, 10 mM); 0.75 mg/mL probe; 10% human serum.

HeLa cells were prepared in 96-well plates for the cytotoxicity studies. Different concentrations of Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs (0.1–0.5 mg/mL), PEI@TPE-SiO2 NPs (0.1–0.5 mg/mL), Ag+ (0–50 μM), mixture of Ag+ (0–50 μM) and PEI@TPE-SiO2 NPs (0.1 mg/mL), and mixture of Ag+ (0–50 μM) and Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs (0.1 mg/mL) were added to the 96-well plate and the cells were cultured at 37 °C in an atmosphere of 5% CO2 for 24 h. Subsequently, the supernatant was removed and 100 μL of MTT phosphate buffer solution (0.5 mg/mL) was added into each well. After incubation for 4 h at 37 °C, excess MTT was removed and 100 μL of DMSO was added to dissolve the formazan, the absorbance density at 550 nm was then measured by a microplate reader.

For fluorescence imaging, the cell culture medium was removed and the cells were washed with phosphate buffer (pH 7.4, 0.1 M) several times. Subsequently, the cells were incubated with the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs (0.1 mg/mL) for 20 min and washed three times with phosphate buffer to remove excess Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs. The cells were then treated with 10 μM and 100 μM H2O2 for 30 min. In order to stain the nucleus, cells were subsequently treated with acridine orange (AO) (2 μg/mL) for 2 min. For the investigation of endogenously generated H2O2, the cells were first incubated with phorbol-12-myristate-13-acetate (PMA) (1 μg/mL) for 90 min, followed by the addition of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs (0.1 mg/mL), and the cells were incubated for 20 min. The cells were washed with phosphate buffer several times to remove the extra Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs. The HeLa cells loaded with the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs were excited at 405 nm, cells stained by AO are excited at 488 nm and emission was collected in the blue channel and green channel using a Nikon C2 confocal laser scanning microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ICP-MS measurements of cellular Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Hela cells were incubated with the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs (0.1 mg/mL) for 20 min, and washed three times with phosphate buffer to remove the excess Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs. The cells were digested with trypsin, and resuspended in phosphate buffer. After that, the cells were counted using a hemocytometer, and centrifuged at 5000 rpm/min for 5 min. Finally, the NPs content in the cells was measured using ICP-MS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs involves three steps: synthesis of the TPE-SiO2 NPs, introduction of polyethyleneimine (PEI), and incorporation of AgNPs on the silica nanoparticles. Initially, silica nanoparticles were prepared according to the Stӧber method (Huang et al., 2017a, b). The tetraphenylethene (TPE) probe was doped into the silica nanoparticles due to the electrostatic interactions between the positively charged TPE probe and the negatively charged silica matrix (Wang et al., 2006). As shown in Fig. S5, TPE probe exhibited strong fluorescence emission peaked at 475 nm after encapsulation into the silica nanoparticles. This visual observation supports that the intramolecular rotations of the AIE luminogen (TPE probe) are restricted by the silica network (Faisal et al., 2010). The fluorescence intensity of the TPE-SiO2 NPs remained almost unchanged after repeated centrifugation, washing and resuspension (Fig. S6a), indicating that leakage of TPE probe from the silica nanoparticles was negligible. Furthermore, the fluorescence of the TPE-SiO2 NPs in the presence of various concentrations of H2O2 remained constant, which was a prerequisite condition for the H2O2 detection (Fig. S6b). The cationic polymer PEI was then introduced onto the surface of the TPE probe doped silica nanoparticles, the fluorescence quantum yield of the PEI@TPE-SiO2 was measured to be 53.3%. Subsequently, AgNPs were synthesized through the reduction of Ag+ ions by formaldehyde using PEI as the template. PEI is a dendrigraft polymer containing chains with primary, secondary and tertiary amino functional groups which can easily chelate with metal ions. Therefore, PEI is an effective template for the synthesis of AgNPs (Yuan et al., 2014b, Qu et al., 2013). UV–vis absorption spectrum of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs showed a peak at 413 nm attributed to the AgNPs (Fig. S7a). There was a clear overlap between the fluorescence spectrum of the TPE-SiO2 NPs and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the AgNPs (Fig. S7b). The fluorescence emission of TPE-SiO2 NPs was quenched effectively by the AgNPs (Fig. S7c). The results suggest that the fluorescence and UV–vis absorption properties of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs were strongly influenced by the AgNPs. The concentration of Ag+ used for the AgNPs synthesis was optimized (Figs. S8, S9, S10). As show in Fig. S8, the fluorescence emission decreased, and the UV–vis absorption increased with increasing Ag+ ions concentration. And 5 mM Ag+ provided the highest fluorescence ratio F/F0 for the detection of H2O2. (Fig. S9). Thus, the optimal concentration of Ag+ for the synthesis of the AgNPs was 5 mM, and the synthesized AgNPs had an average diameter of 15.2 nm (Fig. S10). In addition, the results show that the synthesis of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs is quite reproducible (Fig. S11).

The morphology of the TPE-SiO2 NPs and the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs was characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1, the TPE-SiO2 NPs had a diameter of 83.4 ± 5.4 nm with very smooth surface, while the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs comprised lots of small Ag nanoparticles (15.3 ± 2.8 nm) on the surface of the silica nanoparticles, which was the reason for the effective fluorescence quenching. To further explore the surface composition, XPS spectra were recorded. Fig. 2 shows peaks from all samples with binding energies of 103.5, 154.6, 284.5 and 532.0 eV, which were assigned to Si 2p, Si 2s, C 1s and O 1s signals, respectively (Wang et al., 2017). Furthermore, a new peak at a binding energy of 399.5 eV ascribed to N element (Fukuda et al., 2011) was observed in both spectra of the PEI@TPE-SiO2 NPs and the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs, indicating PEI was introduced onto the silica nanoparticles. In addition, peaks at 368.0 and 374.0 eV observed in the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs sample were attributed to Ag (3d5/2) and Ag (3d3/2), which revealed the metallic state (Ag°) of the AgNPs (Gao et al., 2014) in the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs sample. The surface charge changes of the silica nanoparticles were investigated by zeta potential measurements (Fig. S12). Zeta potential value suggests that the TPE-SiO2 NPs was negatively charged (− 8.2 mV), while the PEI@TPE-SiO2 NPs had positive zeta potential (+ 16.2 mV) due to the existence of amino groups, and the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs also had positively charged surface (+ 18.6 mV). The nanoprobe with positively charged surface is considered advantageous because of its easy translocation across the plasma and endosome membrane of the cell (Chen et al., 2013). For that reason, the presented data corroborate the successful preparation of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs.

The mechanism of our detection strategy involves oxidation of the AgNPs by H2O2, the etching process can be expressed as follows (Chen et al., 2014b):
Ag° + 2H2O2 → Ag+ + O2 •− + 2H2O

Etching of the AgNPs can be clearly seen in TEM images (Fig. S13). After the addition of H2O2, the number of the AgNPs was greatly reduced, and the size of the remaining AgNPs was considerably reduced (9.2 ± 3.7 nm). And because of the AgNPs etching induced by H2O2, some of the AgNPs dropped off from the silica nanoparticles. Almost no AgNPs were observed in the presence of higher concentration of H2O2.

To achieve the best detection performance, some important parameters were optimized, such as the probe concentration (Fig. S14), the buffer solution (Fig. S15) and the reaction time (Fig. S16). Eventually, 0.75 mg/mL was selected as the optimal probe concentration, Tris-HAc (10 mM, 7.4) was chosen as the optimal buffer solution, and the optimal reaction time was 30 min.

The detection of H2O2 was performed under the optimized conditions. As shown in Fig. 3, the addition of H2O2 (1–160 μM) to the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs assay solution induced a significant turn-on fluorescence response at 475 nm. Two linear relationships were obtained between the fluorescence ratio (F/F0) and the concentration of H2O2 in the range of 1–100 μM and 100–160 μM, and the linear regression equations are F/F0 = 0.01[H2O2] + 1.01, and F/F0 = 0.03[H2O2] − 1.06 respectively. The results indicate that the detection at the second stage (100–160 μM) is more sensitive than that at the first stage (1–100 μM). The existence of two linear relations is probably due to the relative high AgNPs etching rate in the presence of higher concentrations of H2O2, thus the fluorescence recovery is more effective. The limit of detection (LOD) for the fluorescence assay was estimated to 0.28 μM. In addition, our method also shows a clear colorimetric signal response. Upon the addition of H2O2 (5–160 μM), the UV–vis absorption decreased gradually, and an obvious color fading of the assay solution was observed with the increase of H2O2 concentration. A linear relationship was obtained between the changes in absorption intensity at 413 nm and the concentration of H2O2 in the range of 5–160 μM. The regression equation is A413 = − 0.0045[H2O2] + 0.92. The detection limit for the colorimetric method was calculated to 2.1 μM.

To evaluate the H2O2 sensing selectivity, a number of possible interfering chemicals (Walekar et al., 2016, Lee et al., 2017) including various heavy metal ions (Ag+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Hg2+), biomolecules (GSH, Cys, Glucose) and ROS (ONOO−,·OH, TBHP, ClO−, O2 •−) were tested. Some heavy metal ions in nature are at low concentrations (Yao et al., 2013; Xuan et al., 2013), thus 200 μM was chosen for the interference study. Intracellular ROS concentrations are estimated to be in the micromolar range (Van der Heijden et al., 2015; Burton et al., 2014), thus 200 μM ROS was chosen. Some biomolecules such as GSH present at high levels in the human body, 10-fold higher H2O2 concentration (2 mM) was thus used for the interference study. Fig. 4 shows that these analytes showed little effect on the fluorescence and UV–vis absorption of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs, and only the addition of H2O2 caused significant assay solution fluorescence and color changes. In addition, some other analytes including various biomolecules, cations, anions, and some strong oxidative reagents were also investigated (Fig. S17). Interestingly, no significant changes in fluorescence signal suggest that the assay strategy is highly selective. While UV–vis absorption shows that the S2O3 2−, I− and S2− ions give some interference, the interference can be easily eliminated by the addition of Ag+ ions, and it is possible to detect H2O2 in the presence of Ag+ ions (Fig. S18). Some ions (such as Fe3+, Fe2+) showed slight influence on the fluorescence emission and UV–vis absorption of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs, thus lower than background signal was detected. Some strong oxidative reagents such as MnO4 −, Br2, Cl2 were also investigated. At lower concentrations, they show little influence. These results clearly demonstrate that the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs can provide a selective fluorometric and colorimetric approach for H2O2 detection.

H2O2 level in human blood was reported to be in the range of 0.25–5 μM, and a high concentration of 30–50 μM was observed in certain disease states or during chronic inflammation (Forman et al., 2016). The practical applicability of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs was investigated by detecting H2O2 in human serum samples. The fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectral changes of the nanoprobe in assay solutions containing 10% human serum and various concentrations of H2O2 were recorded. Fig. S19 shows that the fluorescence ratio increased and the UV–vis absorption intensity at 413 nm decreased proportionally to the increase in H2O2 concentration. In comparison, ABTS was used to detect H2O2 in serum samples, the UV–vis absorption intensity at 421 nm increased with increasing H2O2 concentration, and the performance was comparable with our probe (Fig. S19d). In addition, H2O2 detection in serum was also studied using the commercial Hydrogen Peroxide Detection Kit, and similar response was observed (Fig. S19e). Therefore, the proposed Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs have the potential for the detection of H2O2 in complex biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MTT assay and intracellular imaging of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate cytotoxicity of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs, MTT assay was performed using HeLa cells following the standard protocol. As shown in Fig. S20a, after 24 h of incubation, the cell viability decreased slightly as the concentration of probe increased. The results show that the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs at lower concentration have low cytotoxicity to the cells and can be used for cell imaging studies. In addition, cytotoxicity of PEI@TPE-SiO2 NPs, Ag+, mixture of Ag+ and PEI@TPE-SiO2 NPs, and mixture of Ag+ and Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs was tested. The results show that Ag+ ions at high concentrations did show some cytotoxicity. However, it was observed that in the presence of the NPs, the toxicity of Ag+ ions could be considerably reduced. In order to test the cytotoxicity of the added H2O2, HeLa cells were incubated with 10 and 100 μM H2O2. It can be seen that H2O2 showed little cytotoxicity under our experimental conditions (incubation time: 30 min) (Fig. S21). In addition, the cellular contents of Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs in HeLa cells were determined using ICP-MS (Fig. S22). The cellular uptake of Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs was calculated to be about 12.58 pg/cell.

Under normal physiological conditions, the intracellular H2O2 concentration is estimated to be at 0.05–0.7 μM, and in pathological conditions, the local extracellular concentration of H2O2 can elevate to as high as 10–50 μM (Li et al., 2016). The capability of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs for intracellular imaging of H2O2 was investigated ( Fig. 5). HeLa cells incubated with the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs at 37 °C for 20 min displayed almost no fluorescence in the blue channel. After incubated with 10 μM H2O2, the fluorescence emission can be clearly distinguished from the blank. And the fluorescence emission increased dramatically after exposure to higher concentration of H2O2 (100 μM), which is in agreement with the reported literature works (Wen et al., 2014; Biswas et al., 2017; Chang et al., 2004). In addition, it was known that phorbol myristate acetate (PMA) could activate NADPH-oxidase (Nox) through the redistribution of protein kinase C (PKC) and phosphorylation of several proteins, to induce the endogenous generation of H2O2 through a cellular inflammation response (Karlsson et al., 2000; Xu et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2016). As shown in Fig. 5d, the fluorescence in blue channel increased distinctly after the stimulation with PMA. It was also observed that the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs were mainly distributed in the cytosol (Fig. S23). In addition, the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs were also incubated with the HeLa cells for a prolonged period of time (60 min), the NPs could still be used for the imaging studies, and no reduction in assay performance was observed (Fig. S24). The results clearly demonstrate that the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs show good potential for the intracellular imaging of H2O2.

To investigate the possible influence of Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs aggregation on the sensing of H2O2, the probe solution was dried. The detection of H2O2 was not affected (Fig. S25), thus, the possible aggregation of the NPs should not influence the intracellular imaging of H2O2.

Table S1 compares the detection performance of the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs with some literature reports. Our probe shows distinct advantages: The probe can be used for both colorimetric and turn-on fluorometric detection of H2O2, this dual-mode detection strategy significantly improves the reliability of the assay; The colorimetric method makes it possible for simple detection without the use of any sophisticated instrument, and H2O2 can be easily detected with only naked eye due to the good selectivity of this sensor; The colorimetric method provides broad linear range (5–160 μM), and the fluorescence method has lower LOD (0.28 μM); Some electrochemical methods do have better sensitivity, however, they cannot be used for intracellular imaging related applications. A composite made from N-doped carbon quantum dots and silver nanoparticles was reported for the detection of H2O2 (Walekar et al., 2018). The assay strategy is somewhat similar, however, it should be noted that our probe shows distinct properties, for example, the TPE probe shows excellent aggregation induced fluorescence emission, the sensor shows good selectivity against ROS and biothiols, and it can be used for the detection of H2O2 in human serum and for the intracellular imaging of H2O2.

It should be noted that the colorimetric method may not be very effective in an assay medium with strong background color, the fluorescence method should be used in this situation. In addition, our sensor may also need some further improvements in future work, f﻿o﻿r﻿ ﻿e﻿x﻿a﻿m﻿p﻿l﻿e reduction of the response time (30 min), and better sensitivity would certainly improve the performance of the sensor.

In summary, a colorimetric and turn-on fluorometric dual-readout sensor is developed for the detection of H2O2. A fluorophore-quencher type nanosensor was employed by linking a fluorophore (TPE-SiO2 NPs) with a quencher (AgNPs). The detriment of quencher (AgNPs) by H2O2 led to fluorescence recovery and color fading of the sensor (Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs). Thus, the detection of H2O2 was achieved through visual and fluorescent readout, which makes the sensing more reliable. The sensor exhibited attractive performance for sensitive and selective detection of H2O2. In addition, the Ag@TPE-SiO2 NPs were practically employed for the detection of H2O2 in human serum and intracellular imaging of H2O2. This newly developed sensing methodology could further contribute to the development of next generation dual-readout biosensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21561162004, 51761145102, 21874128), the Science and Technology Development Project (International Collaboration Program) of Jilin Province (20160414040GH).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2018.09.023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Colloids Surf. A: Physicochem. Eng. Asp.
